title: Kubernetes for Developers
summary: Core Kubernetes concepts, deployments, services, configmaps, health probes, resource limits, and rolling updates.

sections.content:
## Kubernetes Core Concepts

Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration platform. Key abstractions:

| Object | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Pod** | Smallest deployable unit. One or more containers sharing network/storage. |
| **Deployment** | Manages a ReplicaSet, handles rolling updates and rollbacks |
| **Service** | Stable network endpoint for a set of Pods (load balancing) |
| **ConfigMap** | Non-sensitive configuration data |
| **Secret** | Sensitive data (passwords, tokens) — base64 encoded |
| **Ingress** | HTTP routing from outside the cluster to Services |
| **HPA** | Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — auto-scale based on CPU/custom metrics |

sections.title: Kubernetes Architecture
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Control Plane
        API[API Server]
        Sched[Scheduler]
        CM[Controller Manager]
        etcd[(etcd)]
    end
    subgraph Worker Node 1
        K1[Kubelet]
        P1[Pod: app-v1]
        P2[Pod: app-v1]
    end
    subgraph Worker Node 2
        K2[Kubelet]
        P3[Pod: app-v1]
    end
    API --> K1
    API --> K2
    K1 --> P1 & P2
    K2 --> P3
    style API fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style etcd fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Deployment YAML

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: order-service
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1        # allow 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0  # never have less than desired replicas
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: order-service
        version: v1.2.0
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-service
        image: registry/order-service:1.2.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
          value: production
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-secret
              key: password
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /actuator/health/readiness
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /actuator/health/liveness
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 60
          periodSeconds: 30
```

## Health Probes

- **Readiness Probe** — "Is this pod ready to receive traffic?" If fails → removed from Service endpoints, no traffic sent
- **Liveness Probe** — "Is this pod still alive?" If fails → container restarted
- **Startup Probe** — "Has the app started yet?" Prevents liveness probe from killing slow-starting apps

```yaml
# Spring Boot Actuator integration
management:
  endpoint:
    health:
      probes:
        enabled: true  # enables /actuator/health/readiness and /liveness
  health:
    livenessstate:
      enabled: true
    readinessstate:
      enabled: true
```

### Marking as not-ready programmatically:
```java
@Autowired
AvailabilityChangeEvent<ReadinessState> readinessEvent;

// During graceful shutdown or maintenance:
AvailabilityChangeEvent.publish(eventPublisher, this, ReadinessState.REFUSING_TRAFFIC);
```

sections.title: Rolling Update Flow
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Before Update
        P1v1[Pod v1] 
        P2v1[Pod v1]
        P3v1[Pod v1]
    end
    subgraph Rolling Update
        P1v2[Pod v2 NEW] 
        P2v1b[Pod v1]
        P3v1b[Pod v1]
    end
    subgraph After Update
        P1v2f[Pod v2]
        P2v2f[Pod v2]
        P3v2f[Pod v2]
    end
    Before_Update --> Rolling_Update
    Rolling_Update --> After_Update
    style P1v2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style P1v2f fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style P2v2f fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style P3v2f fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Resource Requests and Limits

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"   # guaranteed minimum (scheduler uses this)
    cpu: "250m"       # 0.25 CPU core guaranteed
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"   # hard limit — OOMKilled if exceeded
    cpu: "500m"       # throttled if exceeded (not killed)
```

**Critical rule**: always set both requests and limits.
- Without requests: scheduler can't make informed placement decisions
- Without limits: one misbehaving pod can consume all node resources
- Memory limits are hard — exceed them = OOMKilled
- CPU limits are soft — exceed them = throttled (not killed)

## Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: order-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70  # scale when avg CPU > 70%
```

sections.question: What is the difference between a readiness probe and a liveness probe in Kubernetes?
options: Readiness checks if the container started; liveness checks if it's healthy
Readiness controls traffic routing (remove from LB if fails); liveness controls restarts (restart if fails)
Liveness runs at startup; readiness runs during normal operation
They are identical — both restart the pod if they fail
sections.explanation: Readiness probe answers 'should this pod receive traffic?' — failure removes it from Service endpoints but does NOT restart it (e.g. during startup or when dependencies are down). Liveness probe answers 'is this pod alive?' — failure causes Kubernetes to restart the container (e.g. deadlock, memory leak causing unresponsiveness).
